content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_4929293a

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write; transcript includes durable personal event and wedding-planning state/preferences, plus useful context around venue tradeoffs and planner/vendor-selection rationale.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving the stable USER entity; no existing entities provided.
- mention=Michael; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PERSON; name=Michael; reason=Michael is a named person whose engagement party the user attended; no existing entity matches.
- mention=my own wedding / my own wedding plans / your wedding; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's wedding; reason=The user's wedding is a durable planning subject across the transcript.
- mention=Michael's engagement party; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=CONCEPT; name=Michael's engagement party; reason=A specific event mentioned as attended by the user; useful as an object for later claims.
- mention=trendy rooftop bar; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=LOCATION; name=Trendy rooftop bar; reason=A concrete venue/location is mentioned, though unnamed.
- mention=wedding planner / planner; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=CONCEPT; name=Wedding planner; reason=Generic professional role repeatedly discussed as a potential hire; no specific planner is named.
- mention=a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside / countryside venue / B&B; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=LOCATION; name=Countryside bed and breakfast wedding venue; reason=A specific, though unnamed, venue option under consideration.
- mention=a beautiful art museum in the city / city venue / museum; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=LOCATION; name=City art museum wedding venue; reason=A specific, though unnamed, venue option under consideration.
- mention=Green Meadow Farm Catering; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Green Meadow Farm Catering; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=Emily May Photography; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Emily May Photography; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=Petal & Twig; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Petal & Twig; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=City Bites Catering; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=City Bites Catering; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=Urban Lens Photography; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Urban Lens Photography; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=Blooming Beauty; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=Blooming Beauty; reason=Named vendor organization recommended in the planner example.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Michael|wedding|wedding planner|countryside bed and breakfast wedding venue|city art museum wedding venue|Green Meadow Farm Catering|Emily May Photography|Petal & Twig|City Bites Catering|Urban Lens Photography|Blooming Beauty
        text_queries=user planning small wedding ceremony next year venue choice countryside bed and breakfast art museum city|Michael engagement party trendy rooftop bar attended|wedding planner consultation vendor selection countryside versus city vendors|wedding venue preferences small ceremony countryside bed and breakfast art museum
        predicate_hints=has_goal|attended_event|prefers|has_constraint|believes

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:6563b00cc835d931eca2321fe3c0fe3f] EXTERNAL

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/06 (Sat) 04:29
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of planning a small ceremony for my own wedding next year. Can you give me some tips on how to choose the right venue? By the way, I just came back from Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar today, and it got me thinking about my own wedding plans.
assistant: Congratulations on your upcoming wedding planning!

Choosing the right venue is a crucial decision that sets the tone for your special day. I'd be happy to help you with some tips. But first, how exciting that you attended Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar! That must have been a great inspiration for your own wedding planning.

Now, let's dive into the venue selection process. Here are some tips to consider:

1. **Guest list**: Before searching for a venue, finalize your guest list. This will help you determine the size of the venue you need.
2. **Budget**: Set a budget for the venue and stick to it. Consider the cost of the venue, catering, decorations, and other expenses.
3. **Location**: Think about the location of the venue. Is it easily accessible for your guests? Is it in a convenient location for you and your partner?
4. **Ambiance**: Consider the atmosphere you want to create for your wedding. Do you want a formal, elegant setting or a casual, outdoor celebration?
5. **Capacity**: Ensure the venue can accommodate your guest list comfortably. Don't forget to ask about any noise restrictions or curfews.
6. **Amenities**: Think about the amenities you need, such as parking, restrooms, catering services, tables, chairs, and audio-visual equipment.
7. **Catering**: If you have a specific catering style in mind, ensure the venue allows external catering or has in-house options that fit your needs.
8. **Date flexibility**: Be flexible with your dates, as popular venues may have limited availability during peak season.
9. **Reviews and referrals**: Research online reviews, ask for referrals from friends or family, and visit the venue in person to get a sense of the space and service.
10. **Backup plan**: Consider having a backup plan in case of unexpected weather changes or last-minute cancellations.

Some popular venue options to consider:

* Outdoor spaces (parks, gardens, beaches)
* Historic mansions or buildings
* Hotels or resorts
* Restaurants or bars (like the trendy rooftop bar you attended today!)
* Unique venues (museums, art galleries, or warehouses)

Remember to prioritize your needs and preferences, and don't hesitate to ask questions or negotiate with the venue to ensure it's the perfect fit for your special day.

I hope these tips help you get started on your venue search!
user: I'm currently considering two options: a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you have any advice on how to make the final decision between these two venues?
assistant: What a lovely dilemma! Both options sound unique and charming in their own ways. Here are some factors to consider that might help you make a decision that's right for you:

**Cozy Bed and Breakfast in the Countryside:**

* **Intimacy and exclusivity**: A B&B can offer a more intimate and private setting, perfect for a smaller, close-knit gathering.
* **Rustic charm**: The countryside setting can provide a warm, natural ambiance, ideal for a whimsical or vintage-themed wedding.
* **Accommodations**: If the B&B has rooms available, it can be convenient for out-of-town guests or for you and your partner to stay on-site.
* **Food and drink**: A B&B might offer a more personalized, home-cooked meal experience, which could be a nice change of pace from a traditional wedding menu.

However, consider the following:

* **Guest capacity**: B&Bs often have limited space, so you may need to keep your guest list small.
* **Amenities**: Depending on the B&B, you might need to bring in external vendors for services like catering, music, or photography.
* **Accessibility**: The countryside location might be less accessible for guests who don't have cars or prefer not to drive.

**Beautiful Art Museum in the City:**

* **Elegance and sophistication**: An art museum can provide a stunning, cultured backdrop for your ceremony and reception.
* **Capacity and flexibility**: Museums often have larger event spaces, allowing for a bigger guest list and more flexibility with layout and design.
* **Amenities**: Many museums have in-house catering, audio-visual equipment, and other services, making planning easier.
* **Accessibility**: City locations are often more convenient for guests who rely on public transportation or prefer urban amenities.

However, consider the following:

* **Formality**: A museum setting might require a more formal atmosphere, which might not fit your vision.
* **Noise restrictions**: Museums might have noise limitations or strict rules about decorations and activities.
* **Cost**: Renting a museum space can be more expensive than a B&B, especially if you need to hire additional staff or vendors.

Now, ask yourself:

* Which atmosphere and aesthetic do you envision for your wedding? (Rustic charm vs. elegant sophistication)
* How important is intimacy and exclusivity to you? (B&B) vs. a more grand, public celebration (museum)?
* What are your priorities for food, drink, and amenities?
* How will your guests travel and accommodate themselves? (Consider parking, transportation, and nearby hotels)

Take some time to weigh these factors, and trust your instincts. You might also consider visiting both venues again, this time with a more critical eye, to get a better sense of which one feels more like "your" wedding.

I hope this helps you make a decision that's perfect for your special day!
user: I'm also thinking of hiring a wedding planner to help with the logistics. Can you give me some tips on how to find the right one?
assistant: Hiring a wedding planner can be a fantastic decision, especially if you're short on time, want to reduce stress, or need expert guidance. Here are some tips to help you find the right wedding planner for your special day:

1. **Define your needs**: Determine what services you need from a planner. Do you want full-service planning, day-of coordination, or just consulting? This will help you find a planner who specializes in the level of service you require.
2. **Get referrals**: Ask friends, family, or recently married couples for recommendations. They can provide valuable insights and first-hand experiences with the planner.
3. **Check credentials**: Look for planners with professional certifications like the Certified Wedding Planner (CWP) or the Association of Bridal Consultants (ABC). These certifications indicate a level of expertise and commitment to the industry.
4. **Research online**: Browse wedding planning websites, social media, and review platforms like WeddingWire or The Knot. Pay attention to their portfolio, testimonials, and overall aesthetic.
5. **Interview potential planners**: Schedule consultations with 2-3 planners to discuss your vision, budget, and expectations. This will help you gauge their personality, communication style, and expertise.
6. **Ask the right questions**: During the consultation, ask questions like:
	* What's your experience with weddings similar to mine (size, style, budget)?
	* How will you communicate with me throughout the planning process?
	* How do you handle last-minute issues or unexpected changes?
	* Can you provide a detailed breakdown of your services and pricing?
	* Do you have a network of recommended vendors, and will you help me find them?
7. **Check their vendor connections**: A good planner should have an established network of trusted vendors, such as caterers, photographers, and florists. Ask about their vendor relationships and how they can help you find the best fit for your wedding.
8. **Evaluate their organizational skills**: Pay attention to how organized and detail-oriented the planner is during your consultation. This will give you an idea of how they'll handle the logistics of your wedding.
9. **Trust your instincts**: Ultimately, choose a planner who listens to your needs, shares your vision, and makes you feel confident and comfortable.
10. **Review contracts carefully**: Before hiring a planner, make sure you understand their contract, including their services, payment terms, and cancellation policies.

Some additional tips to keep in mind:

* Be clear about your budget and expectations from the start.
* Ensure the planner is available on your wedding date and has a backup plan in place.
* Ask about their experience with weddings in your chosen venue (if you've already selected one).
* Don't hesitate to ask for references or to contact previous clients for feedback.

By following these tips, you'll be well on your way to finding a wedding planner who will help make your special day unforgettable!
user: I'm still deciding between the two venues, but I think I'll schedule a consultation with a wedding planner to get a better sense of how they can help with the logistics. Do you think it's a good idea to have a planner help with finding vendors, or should I try to handle that on my own?
assistant: Scheduling a consultation with a wedding planner is a great idea! They can provide valuable insights and help you navigate the planning process.

Regarding finding vendors, it's a good idea to consider having a planner assist you, especially if you're short on time or not familiar with the local vendor market. Here are some benefits of having a planner help with vendor selection:

**Advantages of having a planner find vendors:**

1. **Time-saving**: A planner can save you hours of research, phone calls, and emails by recommending trusted vendors who fit your style and budget.
2. **Network and connections**: Planners often have an established network of vendors they've worked with before, which can lead to better pricing, priority service, and a smoother planning process.
3. **Quality control**: A planner can help you avoid vendors with poor reputations or those who might not be a good fit for your wedding.
4. **Coordinated logistics**: A planner can ensure that all vendors are aware of the timeline, layout, and other details, reducing the risk of miscommunication or mistakes.
5. **Budget management**: A planner can help you allocate your budget effectively, ensuring you get the best value for your money.

However, if you're the type of person who enjoys researching and finding vendors yourself, or if you have specific vendors in mind, you might want to handle this task on your own. Ultimately, it's up to you to decide how much involvement you want from your planner.

**When to handle vendor selection on your own:**

1. **You have a clear vision**: If you have a specific style or aesthetic in mind, you might want to search for vendors that fit your vision.
2. **You have existing connections**: If you have friends or family members who are vendors or know someone who can recommend a great vendor, you might want to explore those options.
3. **You're on a tight budget**: If you're working with a very limited budget, you might need to search for vendors that fit within your budget constraints.

During your consultation with the wedding planner, discuss your preferences and expectations regarding vendor selection. They can help you determine the best approach based on your needs and their services.

Remember, a good planner will work with you to understand your vision and preferences, so don't hesitate to ask for their input and guidance throughout the planning process!
user: I think it would be helpful to get the planner's input on the vendor selection process, especially since I'm still deciding between the two venues. Can you ask the planner about their experience with vendors in the countryside versus the city, and how they think it might impact the overall planning process?
assistant: Here's a potential question you could ask the planner:

"I'm still deciding between a countryside venue and a city venue, and I'm wondering how you think that might impact the vendor selection process. Have you worked with vendors in both rural and urban areas, and are there any specific challenges or advantages to consider when working with vendors in each location?"

This question can help you gauge the planner's experience and expertise in working with vendors in different locations, as well as their ability to think critically about the potential challenges and opportunities that come with each venue.

Here's an example response from the planner:

"Ah, great question! I've worked with vendors in both the countryside and the city, and each presents its own unique set of challenges and opportunities. In the countryside, vendors may be more limited in terms of selection and availability, but they often have a more personalized approach and can offer a more intimate, bespoke experience. On the other hand, city vendors tend to be more abundant and offer a wider range of options, but they may be more expensive and have more strict logistical requirements.

"In terms of logistics, countryside vendors may require more planning and coordination due to their remote location, whereas city vendors may have more flexibility in terms of transportation and delivery. Additionally, countryside vendors may have more limited access to resources and equipment, whereas city vendors may have more options for rentals and supplies.

"That being said, I've worked with some amazing vendors in both locations, and with careful planning and communication, we can make either option work beautifully. If you're considering a countryside venue, I would recommend starting the vendor search process a bit earlier to ensure we can find the right fit, and being flexible with our expectations in terms of availability and logistics. If you're considering a city venue, we can take advantage of the abundance of vendors and options to create a more elaborate and detailed event.

"Ultimately, the key is to find vendors who are a good fit for your vision and budget, regardless of the location. I'd be happy to help you research and recommend vendors for either option, and work with you to create a seamless and stress-free planning process."

By asking this question, you can get a better sense of the planner's experience and expertise, as well as their ability to think critically about the potential challenges and opportunities that come with each venue. This can help you make a more informed decision about which venue to choose, and how to approach the vendor selection process.
user: Can you give me some examples of vendors you've worked with in the countryside versus the city? For example, have you worked with any specific caterers, photographers, or florists in these areas that you would recommend?
assistant: Here's an example response from the planner:

"Absolutely! I'd be happy to share some examples of vendors I've worked with in the countryside and city.

"In the countryside, I've had the pleasure of working with some incredible vendors who really understand the unique needs of a rural wedding. For catering, I highly recommend Green Meadow Farm Catering, who offer farm-to-table cuisine and can accommodate a wide range of dietary needs. They're based in the countryside and have a deep understanding of the local food scene.

"For photography, I've worked with Emily May Photography, who has a beautiful, rustic style that's perfect for capturing the charm of a countryside wedding. She's based in the nearby town and knows the area like the back of her hand.

"For florals, I've worked with Petal & Twig, who source their flowers from local farms and create stunning, whimsical arrangements that are perfect for a countryside wedding. They're based in the countryside and have a deep understanding of the local flora and fauna.

"In the city, I've worked with a wide range of vendors who offer a more modern, urban aesthetic. For catering, I recommend City Bites Catering, who offer a range of cuisines and can accommodate large groups. They're based in the city and have a team of experienced chefs and event coordinators.

"For photography, I've worked with Urban Lens Photography, who have a sleek, modern style that's perfect for capturing the energy of a city wedding. They're based in the city and have a deep understanding of the urban landscape.

"For florals, I've worked with Blooming Beauty, who offer a range of styles and can create everything from modern, structural arrangements to lush, romantic bouquets. They're based in the city and have a team of experienced florists who can handle large events.

"Of course, these are just a few examples of the many talented vendors I've worked with in both the countryside and city. But I think they give you a sense of the types of vendors I've worked with and the level of quality and service you can expect."

This response gives you a better sense of the planner's experience working with vendors in both the countryside and city, and provides specific examples of vendors they've worked with in the past. It also gives you a sense of the planner's taste and style, and helps you determine whether they're a good fit for your wedding.

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or

- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so

  retrieved memory.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of planning a small ceremony for my own wedding next year. Can you give me some tips on how to choose the right venue? By the way, I just came back from Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar today, and it got me thinking about my own wedding plans.
assistant: Congratulations on your upcoming wedding planning!

Choosing the right venue is a crucial decision that sets the tone for your special day. I'd be happy to help you with some tips. But first, how exciting that you attended Michael's engagement party at a trendy rooftop bar! That must have been a great inspiration for your own wedding planning.

Now, let's dive into the venue selection process. Here are some tips to consider:

1. **Guest list**: Before searching for a venue, finalize your guest list. This will help you determine the size of the venue you need.
2. **Budget**: Set a budget for the venue and stick to it. Consider the cost of the venue, catering, decorations, and other expenses.
3. **Location**: Think about the location of the venue. Is it easily accessible for your guests? Is it in a convenient location for you and your partner?
4. **Ambiance**: Consider the atmosphere you want to create for your wedding. Do you want a formal, elegant setting or a casual, outdoor celebration?
5. **Capacity**: Ensure the venue can accommodate your guest list comfortably. Don't forget to ask about any noise restrictions or curfews.
6. **Amenities**: Think about the amenities you need, such as parking, restrooms, catering services, tables, chairs, and audio-visual equipment.
7. **Catering**: If you have a specific catering style in mind, ensure the venue allows external catering or has in-house options that fit your needs.
8. **Date flexibility**: Be flexible with your dates, as popular venues may have limited availability during peak season.
9. **Reviews and referrals**: Research online reviews, ask for referrals from friends or family, and visit the venue in person to get a sense of the space and service.
10. **Backup plan**: Consider having a backup plan in case of unexpected weather changes or last-minute cancellations.

Some popular venue options to consider:

* Outdoor spaces (parks, gardens, beaches)
* Historic mansions or buildings
* Hotels or resorts
* Restaurants or bars (like the trendy rooftop bar you attended today!)
* Unique venues (museums, art galleries, or warehouses)

Remember to prioritize your needs and preferences, and don't hesitate to ask questions or negotiate with the venue to ensure it's the perfect fit for your special day.

I hope these tips help you get started on your venue search!
user: I'm currently considering two options: a cozy bed and breakfast in the countryside or a beautiful art museum in the city. Do you have any advice on how to make the final decision between these two venues?
assistant: What a lovely dilemma! Both options sound unique and charming in their own ways. Here are some factors to consider that might help you make a decision that's right for you:

**Cozy Bed and Breakfast in the Countryside:**

* **Intimacy and exclusivity**: A B&B can offer a more intimate and private setting, perfect for a smaller, close-knit gathering.
* **Rustic charm**: The countryside setting can provide a warm, natural ambiance, ideal for a whimsical or vintage-themed wedding.
* **Accommodations**: If the B&B has rooms available, it can be convenient for out-of-town guests or for you and your partner to stay on-site.
* **Food and drink**: A B&B might offer a more personalized, home-cooked meal experience, which could be a nice change of pace from a traditional wedding menu.

However, consider the following:

* **Guest capacity**: B&Bs often have limited space, so you may need to keep your guest list small.
* **Amenities**: Depending on the B&B, you might need to bring in external vendors for services like catering, music, or photography.
* **Accessibility**: The countryside location might be less accessible for guests who don't have cars or prefer not to drive.

**Beautiful Art Museum in the City:**

* **Elegance and sophistication**: An art museum can provide a stunning, cultured backdrop for your ceremony and reception.
* **Capacity and flexibility**: Museums often have larger event spaces, allowing for a bigger guest list and more flexibility with layout and design.
* **Amenities**: Many museums have in-house catering, audio-visual equipment, and other services, making planning easier.
* **Accessibility**: City locations are often more convenient for guests who rely on public transportation or prefer urban amenities.

However, consider the following:

* **Formality**: A museum setting might require a more formal atmosphere, which might not fit your vision.
* **Noise restrictions**: Museums might have noise limitations or strict rules about decorations and activities.
* **Cost**: Renting a museum space can be more expensive than a B&B, especially if you need to hire additional staff or vendors.

Now, ask yourself:

* Which atmosphere and aesthetic do you envision for your wedding? (Rustic charm vs. elegant sophistication)
* How important is intimacy and exclusivity to you? (B&B) vs. a more grand, public celebration (museum)?
* What are your priorities for food, drink, and amenities?
* How will your guests travel and accommodate themselves? (Consider parking, transportation, and nearby hotels)

Take some time to weigh these factors, and trust your instincts. You might also consider visiting both venues again, this time with a more critical eye, to get a better sense of which one feels more like "your" wedding.

I hope this helps you make a decision that's perfect for your special day!
user: I'm also thinking of hiring a wedding planner to help with the logistics. Can you give me some tips on how to find the right one?
assistant: Hiring a wedding planner can be a fantastic decision, especially if you're short on time, want to reduce stress, or need expert guidance. Here are some tips to help you find the right wedding planner for your special day:

1. **Define your needs**: Determine what services you need from a planner. Do you want full-service planning, day-of coordination, or just consulting? This will help you find a planner who specializes in the level of service you require.
2. **Get referrals**: Ask friends, family, or recently married couples for recommendations. They can provide valuable insights and first-hand experiences with the planner.
3. **Check credentials**: Look for planners with professional certifications like the Certified Wedding Planner (CWP) or the Association of Bridal Consultants (ABC). These certifications indicate a level of expertise and commitment to the industry.
4. **Research online**: Browse wedding planning websites, social media, and review platforms like WeddingWire or The Knot. Pay attention to their portfolio, testimonials, and overall aesthetic.
5. **Interview potential planners**: Schedule consultations with 2-3 planners to discuss your vision, budget, and expectations. This will help you gauge their personality, communication style, and expertise.
6. **Ask the right questions**: During the consultation, ask questions like:
	* What's your experience with weddings similar to mine (size, style, budget)?
	* How will you communicate with me throughout the planning process?
	* How do you handle last-minute issues or unexpected changes?
	* Can you provide a detailed breakdown of your services and pricing?
	* Do you have a network of recommended vendors, and will you help me find them?
7. **Check their vendor connections**: A good planner should have an established network of trusted vendors, such as caterers, photographers, and florists. Ask about their vendor relationships and how they can help you find the best fit for your wedding.
8. **Evaluate their organizational skills**: Pay attention to how organized and detail-oriented the planner is during your consultation. This will give you an idea of how they'll handle the logistics of your wedding.
9. **Trust your instincts**: Ultimately, choose a planner who listens to your needs, shares your vision, and makes you feel confident and comfortable.
10. **Review contracts carefully**: Before hiring a planner, make sure you understand their contract, including their services, payment terms, and cancellation policies.

Some additional tips to keep in mind:

* Be clear about your budget and expectations from the start.
* Ensure the planner is available on your wedding date and has a backup plan in place.
* Ask about their experience with weddings in your chosen venue (if you've already selected one).
* Don't hesitate to ask for references or to contact previous clients for feedback.

By following these tips, you'll be well on your way to finding a wedding planner who will help make your special day unforgettable!
user: I'm still deciding between the two venues, but I think I'll schedule a consultation with a wedding planner to get a better sense of how they can help with the logistics. Do you think it's a good idea to have a planner help with finding vendors, or should I try to handle that on my own?
assistant: Scheduling a consultation with a wedding planner is a great idea! They can provide valuable insights and help you navigate the planning process.

Regarding finding vendors, it's a good idea to consider having a planner assist you, especially if you're short on time or not familiar with the local vendor market. Here are some benefits of having a planner help with vendor selection:

**Advantages of having a planner find vendors:**

1. **Time-saving**: A planner can save you hours of research, phone calls, and emails by recommending trusted vendors who fit your style and budget.
2. **Network and connections**: Planners often have an established network of vendors they've worked with before, which can lead to better pricing, priority service, and a smoother planning process.
3. **Quality control**: A planner can help you avoid vendors with poor reputations or those who might not be a good fit for your wedding.
4. **Coordinated logistics**: A planner can ensure that all vendors are aware of the timeline, layout, and other details, reducing the risk of miscommunication or mistakes.
5. **Budget management**: A planner can help you allocate your budget effectively, ensuring you get the best value for your money.

However, if you're the type of person who enjoys researching and finding vendors yourself, or if you have specific vendors in mind, you might want to handle this task on your own. Ultimately, it's up to you to decide how much involvement you want from your planner.

**When to handle vendor selection on your own:**

1. **You have a clear vision**: If you have a specific style or aesthetic in mind, you might want to search for vendors that fit your vision.
2. **You have existing connections**: If you have friends or family members who are vendors or know someone who can recommend a great vendor, you might want to explore those options.
3. **You're on a tight budget**: If you're working with a very limited budget, you might need to search for vendors that fit within your budget constraints.

During your consultation with the wedding planner, discuss your preferences and expectations regarding vendor selection. They can help you determine the best approach based on your needs and their services.

Remember, a good planner will work with you to understand your vision and preferences, so don't hesitate to ask for their input and guidance throughout the planning process!
user: I think it would be helpful to get the planner's input on the vendor selection process, especially since I'm still deciding between the two venues. Can you ask the planner about their experience with vendors in the countryside versus the city, and how they think it might impact the overall planning process?
assistant: Here's a potential question you could ask the planner:

"I'm still deciding between a countryside venue and a city venue, and I'm wondering how you think that might impact the vendor selection process. Have you worked with vendors in both rural and urban areas, and are there any specific challenges or advantages to consider when working with vendors in each location?"

This question can help you gauge the planner's experience and expertise in working with vendors in different locations, as well as their ability to think critically about the potential challenges and opportunities that come with each venue.

Here's an example response from the planner:

"Ah, great question! I've worked with vendors in both the countryside and the city, and each presents its own unique set of challenges and opportunities. In the countryside, vendors may be more limited in terms of selection and availability, but they often have a more personalized approach and can offer a more intimate, bespoke experience. On the other hand, city vendors tend to be more abundant and offer a wider range of options, but they may be more expensive and have more strict logistical requirements.

"In terms of logistics, countryside vendors may require more planning and coordination due to their remote location, whereas city vendors may have more flexibility in terms of transportation and delivery. Additionally, countryside vendors may have more limited access to resources and equipment, whereas city vendors may have more options for rentals and supplies.

"That being said, I've worked with some amazing vendors in both locations, and with careful planning and communication, we can make either option work beautifully. If you're considering a countryside venue, I would recommend starting the vendor search process a bit earlier to ensure we can find the right fit, and being flexible with our expectations in terms of availability and logistics. If you're considering a city venue, we can take advantage of the abundance of vendors and options to create a more elaborate and detailed event.

"Ultimately, the key is to find vendors who are a good fit for your vision and budget, regardless of the location. I'd be happy to help you research and recommend vendors for either option, and work with you to create a seamless and stress-free planning process."

By asking this question, you can get a better sense of the planner's experience and expertise, as well as their ability to think critically about the potential challenges and opportunities that come with each venue. This can help you make a more informed decision about which venue to choose, and how to approach the vendor selection process.
user: Can you give me some examples of vendors you've worked with in the countryside versus the city? For example, have you worked with any specific caterers, photographers, or florists in these areas that you would recommend?
assistant: Here's an example response from the planner:

"Absolutely! I'd be happy to share some examples of vendors I've worked with in the countryside and city.

"In the countryside, I've had the pleasure of working with some incredible vendors who really understand the unique needs of a rural wedding. For catering, I highly recommend Green Meadow Farm Catering, who offer farm-to-table cuisine and can accommodate a wide range of dietary needs. They're based in the countryside and have a deep understanding of the local food scene.

"For photography, I've worked with Emily May Photography, who has a beautiful, rustic style that's perfect for capturing the charm of a countryside wedding. She's based in the nearby town and knows the area like the back of her hand.

"For florals, I've worked with Petal & Twig, who source their flowers from local farms and create stunning, whimsical arrangements that are perfect for a countryside wedding. They're based in the countryside and have a deep understanding of the local flora and fauna.

"In the city, I've worked with a wide range of vendors who offer a more modern, urban aesthetic. For catering, I recommend City Bites Catering, who offer a range of cuisines and can accommodate large groups. They're based in the city and have a team of experienced chefs and event coordinators.

"For photography, I've worked with Urban Lens Photography, who have a sleek, modern style that's perfect for capturing the energy of a city wedding. They're based in the city and have a deep understanding of the urban landscape.

"For florals, I've worked with Blooming Beauty, who offer a range of styles and can create everything from modern, structural arrangements to lush, romantic bouquets. They're based in the city and have a team of experienced florists who can handle large events.

"Of course, these are just a few examples of the many talented vendors I've worked with in both the countryside and city. But I think they give you a sense of the types of vendors I've worked with and the level of quality and service you can expect."

This response gives you a better sense of the planner's experience working with vendors in both the countryside and city, and provides specific examples of vendors they've worked with in the past. It also gives you a sense of the planner's taste and style, and helps you determine whether they're a good fit for your wedding.
